text:
A documentary on Detroit's bid to host the Summer Olympic Games in 1968 is being released to coincide with the 2024 games now underway in Paris.

Detroit's Olympic Uprisingchronicles the divided reaction to the city's Olympic quest.

Writer and producer Silke Weineck said one point the documentary hopes to make is that 1963 — the year the bid for the 1968 Olympics was made — was a watershed moment that didn't happen.

"The bid for the Olympics was a chance to really rethink how the city worked and to finally make it more just," Weineck said onWindsor Morning.

"One problem with the bid was that the Black community was not sufficiently involved and one young group of activists — and that's kind of the centre of our film — decides to mount a protest, saying Detroit is not worthy to host an event dedicated to universal brotherhood."

Weineck said the film reveals the tensions between the people who were saying "Detroit [is the] best city on earth, we must have the Olympics here," and those who were saying, "wait a minute."

According to Weineck, the documentary includes "some fabulous historic footage," including United States senator Mitt Romney's father, George Romney, who was the governor of Michigan, going all in on the bid. There's also footage of Frederick Matthaei, an industrialist and the driving figure behind all of the bids starting in the late 30s, she said.

"We show these guys hustling and boosting Detroit and being very, very happy about having now mounted the best bid in history, they're pretty sure they're going to get it this time," Weineck said.

"We talk a little bit about what Detroit was like in the early 60s … and we're trying to build that contrast between, on the one hand, an industrial city starting its decline, but still very wealthy, and the Black population, which suffers from housing discrimination, economic inequality [and] police brutality."

Weineck said two events in the summer of 1963 negatively impacted Detroit's bid. First, Cynthia Scott — a 24-year-old African American woman — was shot on the streets by a policeman, Theodore Spicher. And while it wasn't the first police killing in Detroit, it "really galvanized people because it was covered up within one day."

Secondly, she said, just before the Detroit delegation leaves for Germany to present their bid to the International Olympic Committee, city council voted seven to two to maintain formal racial segregation in housing.

"It was shocking. And so people were really, really mad. So when they go to Baden-Baden, they thought they would go and triumph, but … there were a lot of letters from Black Detroiters to the International Olympic Committee saying this city is not ready."

Detroit has made a total of nine bids to host the Olympics: 1940, 1944, 1948, 1952, 1956, 1960, 1964, 1968, 1972.

Stefan Szymanski, the film's director, said Detroit was one of the most iconic cities on the planet in 1963, so it was not an oddity that people thought it was natural that this city might reasonably expect to host the Olympic Games.



"The question is why the bid actually failed… It's the Motor City, it's Motown. Motown music is the beat of America, the beat of the world," Szymanski said, adding that part of the failure had to do with the civil rights protests that were happening in the city.

"Detroit was one of the main centres for the emerging civil rights movement in the 1960s, but it's also to do with Olympic politics, and oddly, the president of the IOC, Avery Brundage, who was born in Detroit but seemed to have hated the city and seemed to have been determined to derail its bid," Szymanski said.

Detroit's WBJK TV (channel 2) will be showing and streaming the documentary at 2 p.m. ET on Saturday.

Weineck said showings are also planned for various libraries and community centres.

Web Writer / Editor

Desmond Brown is a GTA-based writer and editor who covers stories for various CBC bureaus in Ontario. In 2025 Desmond won the National Association of Black Journalists Salute to Excellence Award in Digital Media – International Reporting: Single Story News.

With files from Windsor Morning

title: Documentary chronicles Detroit's failed bid to host 1968 Olympic Games | CBC News
description: A documentary on Detroit’s bid to host the Summer Olympic Games in 1968 is being released to coincide with the 2024 games now underway in Paris.
